name: Top-Down Camera
description: A camera mode that views the play space from above or at an angled overhead perspective while tracking targets within screen-space bounds.
design_notes: Top-down camera design should define camera_target_id, zoom level, screen-space bounds, occlusion policy, input-relative movement, and camera limits. The view must expose threats and navigation routes while keeping player orientation, verticality, and indoor occlusion readable.
design_purpose: Show tactical space, enemy positions, navigation routes, and interaction zones from a readable overhead view.
Support movement and aiming that are relative to screen, world, cursor, or controller direction.
Give designers stable framing for arenas, rooms, puzzles, and strategy-like layouts.
player_fantasy: The player feels aware of the surrounding play space and can plan movement or actions from a clear overhead perspective.

parameters.name: camera_target_id
parameters.description: Target entity or group that anchors the overhead view.

parameters.name: top_down_pitch_degrees
parameters.description: Camera pitch used for vertical or angled overhead composition.

parameters.name: zoom_level
parameters.description: Current zoom scalar for world-to-screen scale.

parameters.name: screen_space_bounds
parameters.description: Screen margin that target movement can occupy before camera follows.

parameters.name: camera_occlusion_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule for handling roofs, walls, foliage, or tall objects above the player.

parameters.name: input_relative_policy_id
parameters.description: How movement or aiming input maps to the top-down view.

edge_cases: Tall walls, roofs, trees, and bridges can hide the player or target unless occlusion policy is explicit.
Mouse/cursor aiming and gamepad aiming may need different camera_target_id or cursor weighting rules.
Zooming out can reveal spoiler areas, hidden rooms, or enemies behind fog-of-war bounds.
Vertical layers such as stairs, flying enemies, or basements can be ambiguous from overhead view.
common_bugs: Camera follows raw player position and jitters when the character collides with dense geometry.
Input-relative movement changes when the camera yaw or map rotation changes without remapping controls.
Occlusion fades the wrong roof or leaves invisible collision blocking the view.
Screen-space bounds ignore UI safe areas and place the player under HUD panels.
balancing_notes: Wider zoom improves planning but can reduce surprise and reveal encounter setup.
Strict screen-space bounds make the camera stable; loose bounds make fast movement feel more responsive.
Top-down views often require enemy telegraphs and projectiles to be readable at smaller screen sizes.
accessibility_notes: Provide zoom options, high-contrast outlines, and reduced camera smoothing for players who need clearer spatial tracking.
Avoid critical information hidden only by vertical depth cues; use shadows, labels, or outlines.

implementation_notes.unity: Use orthographic or angled perspective cameras with a target group and screen-space dead zone. Occlusion volumes or roof fade components should use camera_occlusion_policy_id, and input mapping should read input_relative_policy_id.
implementation_notes.godot: Use Camera2D or Camera3D with explicit zoom and bounds. For tile or room games, clamp to authored camera limits and drive roof fading or outlines from visibility areas.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use a spring arm or top-down camera component with collision and roof/foliage fade rules. Enhanced Input should map movement through the chosen screen, world, or camera-relative policy.
implementation_notes.web: Represent top-down camera state with target id, zoom, bounds, and occlusion policy. Canvas, Phaser, or Three.js renderers should apply safe-area-aware screen bounds before projecting UI markers.

relationships.reason: Top-down strategy views often pair with fog-of-war cells and minimap visibility state.
Top-down cameras and minimaps can duplicate spatial information unless marker filtering and zoom levels are coordinated.
Top-down visibility to the player may reveal areas that AI line-of-sight rules still treat as hidden.

example_games.title: Hades
example_games.note: Uses an angled top-down view for combat readability.

example_games.title: Hotline Miami
example_games.note: Uses top-down framing for fast combat and room awareness.

example_games.title: Diablo III
example_games.note: Uses top-down camera framing for combat readability and enemy positioning.
